questions.question_text: Under an exclusive right-to-sell listing in Alaska, when is the broker entitled to a commission?
questions.correct_answer: Regardless of who finds the buyer
distractors: Only if the broker personally finds the buyer
Only if another licensed agent finds the buyer
Only if the seller does not find the buyer themselves
questions.explanation: An exclusive right-to-sell listing entitles the broker to a commission regardless of who procures the buyer — whether it's the listing broker, another agent, or even the seller themselves. This provides the broadest protection for the listing broker.

questions.question_text: A seller has an exclusive agency listing. The seller personally finds a buyer without any agent involvement. Does the listing broker earn a commission?
questions.correct_answer: No, the seller is not obligated to pay the broker
distractors: Yes, the broker earns a full commission
Yes, but only a reduced commission rate
No, but the seller must reimburse marketing costs
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive agency listing, the broker earns a commission unless the seller personally finds the buyer without any broker assistance. This is the key difference from an exclusive right-to-sell, where the broker is paid regardless.

questions.question_text: A property owner gives open listings to three different brokerages. Which broker earns the commission?
questions.correct_answer: Only the broker who produces the buyer
distractors: All three brokers split the commission equally
The first broker to list the property
The broker with the highest asking price
questions.explanation: With an open listing, only the broker who actually procures the buyer earns the commission. It is non-exclusive, meaning the seller can work with multiple brokers and owes a commission only to the one who brings a successful buyer.

questions.question_text: A seller wants maximum broker effort and marketing but also wants to find their own buyer. Which listing type best fits this scenario?
distractors: Exclusive right-to-sell listing
Open listing
Net listing
questions.explanation: An exclusive agency listing provides broker motivation through exclusivity (no competing brokers) while preserving the seller's right to sell independently without paying a commission. It balances broker incentive with seller flexibility.

questions.question_text: Which listing type gives the broker the greatest assurance of earning a commission in Alaska?
distractors: Exclusive agency
Open listing
Buyer representation agreement
questions.explanation: The exclusive right-to-sell gives the broker the greatest commission protection because the broker earns a commission regardless of who finds the buyer — even if the seller procures their own buyer. This is why it's the most commonly used listing type.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a required element of a valid exclusive listing agreement in Alaska?
distractors: The commission rate set by AREC
The buyer's pre-approval amount
The listing agent's license number
questions.explanation: Under AS 08.88.341, exclusive listings and management contracts must have a definite expiration date, renewable only by a written agreement signed by the client. All listings must be in writing and signed. There is no AREC-set commission rate — rates are negotiable.

questions.question_text: A broker presents a listing agreement to a seller that does not include a property description. Is this agreement valid?
questions.correct_answer: No, a property description is a required element
distractors: Yes, the property description can be added later
Yes, if the MLS listing has the description
No, but only if the property is vacant land
questions.explanation: A listing is an employment contract that must identify the property it covers; without a property description the agreement fails to define its subject matter. AS 08.88.341 separately requires all listings to be in writing and signed, and exclusive listings to have a definite expiration date.

questions.question_text: An Alaska listing agreement must include disclosure of which of the following?
questions.correct_answer: The licensee's relationship with the parties
distractors: The seller's remaining mortgage balance
The estimated closing costs for the buyer
The property's assessed tax value
questions.explanation: Alaska listing agreements must contain the licensee's relationship disclosure, informing the seller about the type of representation being provided. This is consistent with Alaska's requirement for written disclosure at the start of specific assistance.

questions.question_text: Must an Alaska listing agreement be in writing?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, listing agreements must be in writing
distractors: No, verbal listings are valid for up to 30 days
Only for properties valued above $100,000
Only for exclusive listings, not open listings
questions.explanation: Alaska listing agreements must be in writing to be enforceable. This requirement applies to all types of listing agreements and ensures that the terms — including compensation, duration, and relationship disclosure — are clearly documented.

questions.question_text: An exclusive right-to-sell listing agreement without an expiration date is presented to a seller. What is the status of this agreement?
questions.correct_answer: It is missing a required element
distractors: It is valid but defaults to 6 months
It is valid and renews automatically
It is valid until the seller cancels in writing
questions.explanation: Under AS 08.88.341, an exclusive listing must have a definite expiration date, and the listing may be renewed or extended only by a written agreement signed by the client. Without one, the agreement is missing a statutorily required element. This protects sellers from being locked into indefinite obligations with a broker.

questions.question_text: A listing expires on March 15. On March 20, a buyer who first saw the property during the listing period makes an offer. Can the broker claim a commission?
questions.correct_answer: Only if the listing contains a broker protection clause
distractors: Yes, the broker always earns a commission for introductions
No, the listing has expired and no commission is owed
Yes, but only if the buyer submits a full-price offer
questions.explanation: A broker protection clause (also called an override or safety clause) provides the broker with commission protection for a specified period after the listing expires, for buyers who were introduced during the listing. Without such a clause, the expired listing ends the broker's commission entitlement.

questions.question_text: Which event would typically terminate a listing agreement in Alaska?
questions.correct_answer: Mutual agreement between the broker and seller
distractors: The seller receiving any offer on the property
The broker going on vacation for two weeks
A competing brokerage listing a similar property
questions.explanation: A listing can be terminated by mutual agreement of the broker and seller, by expiration of the listing term, by completion of the sale, or by other legal grounds (e.g., death of the principal broker). Receiving an offer or competitor activity does not terminate a listing.

questions.question_text: A seller asks the listing broker to cancel an exclusive right-to-sell listing before the expiration date. What should the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Negotiate a mutual release or termination agreement
distractors: Refuse, as the listing cannot be cancelled early
Immediately release the listing without conditions
File a complaint with AREC against the seller
questions.explanation: A listing agreement is a contract, and early termination requires agreement from both parties. The broker should negotiate a mutual release. The seller cannot unilaterally cancel without potential liability, and the broker cannot simply refuse to discuss termination.

questions.question_text: A listing agreement specifies a listing price of $350,000. The seller later wants to reduce the price to $325,000. How should this change be handled?
questions.correct_answer: By executing a written amendment to the listing
distractors: By verbal agreement between the seller and broker
By filing a price change notice with AREC
The listing must be cancelled and a new one created
questions.explanation: Changes to the listing price should be documented through a written amendment to the listing agreement. Verbal changes lack the documentation needed for clarity and enforceability. A new listing is not necessary — an amendment suffices.

questions.question_text: If the principal broker of a listing brokerage dies, what happens to the brokerage's listings?
questions.correct_answer: The listings may terminate depending on the circumstances
distractors: All listings automatically transfer to the associate broker
All listings remain active for 90 days
AREC assigns a temporary broker to manage them
questions.explanation: Death of the principal broker can terminate listing agreements, as the broker was a party to the contract. The outcome depends on the specific terms of the listing, whether the brokerage has other principals, and applicable law regarding contract termination upon death.
